perturbation.original_text: Upon written demand by the Disclosing Party , the Receiving Party shall: (i) cease using the Confidential Information; (ii) promptly return the Confidential Information and all copies, notes or extracts thereof to the Disclosing Party within seven (7)da ys of receipt of demand; and/or (iii) upon request of the Disclosing Party, destroy all such Confidential Information, including copies thereof, and shall furnish the Disclosing Party with written certification of destruction.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon written demand by the Disclosing Party , the Receiving Party shall: (i) cease using the Confidential Information; (ii) promptly return the Confidential Information and all copies, notes or extracts thereof to the Disclosing Party within one hundred and twenty (120) days of receipt of demand; and/or (iii) upon request of the Disclosing Party, destroy all such Confidential Information, including copies thereof, and shall furnish the Disclosing Party with written certification of destruction.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return deadline from 7 days to 120 days is unusually long and potentially unreasonable, especially if 'YOUR COMPANY STATE' is California. California law implies a duty to act in good faith and deal fairly, and unreasonably delaying the return of confidential information could violate this.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1708 states that every person is bound, without contract, to abstain from injuring the person or property of another, or infringing upon any of his or her rights. This can be contradicted by setting an unreasonably long deadline (120 days) for the return of confidential information after a demand, potentially harming the Disclosing Party by delaying their access to their proprietary information, or by infringing upon their rights.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1708. Every person is bound, without contract, to abstain from injuring the person or property of another, or infringing upon any of his or her rights. (Amended by Stats. 2002, Ch. 664, Sec. 38.5. Effective January 1, 2003.)
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces an inconsistency regarding the timeframe for returning confidential information, potentially contradicting the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under California law. The original text specifies a 7-day return period, while the changed text extends this to 120 days. The Law Explanation states that setting an unreasonably long deadline (120 days) for the return of confidential information after a demand could potentially harm the Disclosing Party by delaying their access to their proprietary information, or by infringing upon their rights. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Civil Code § 1708, states that every person is bound, without contract, to abstain from injuring the person or property of another, or infringing upon any of his or her rights. The change to 120 days could be construed as infringing upon the Disclosing Party's rights to their confidential information and potentially injuring them due to the delay.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect for a period of five (5) years from the Effective Date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: Removing a defined term of five years and replacing it with an indefinite term may be problematic under California law, as it could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade, depending on the nature of the confidential information and the relationship between the parties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. While NDAs are generally permissible, an indefinite term for confidentiality could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint if it unduly restricts a party's ability to engage in their profession or trade at some point in the future.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the agreement from a fixed term of five years to an indefinite term. According to the Law Explanation, this may violate California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which states that contracts restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession are void. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, stating that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The Perturbation Explanation suggests this could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade, depending on the specifics. Because the altered text introduces a potentially indefinite restraint, contradicting the statute protecting freedom to engage in a profession, there is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice, approval, request, authorization, direction or other communication required or permitted under this Agreement shall be given in writing and shall be deemed to have been delivered: (i) one (1) business day after deposit with a commercial overnight courier with written verification of receipt; or (ii) five (5) business days after the mailing date if sent by U.S. mail, return receipt requested, postage and charges prepaid.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice, approval, request, authorization, direction or other communication required or permitted under this Agreement shall be given orally and shall be deemed to have been delivered immediately upon utterance.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring oral notice only, with immediate delivery, contradicts the Statute of Frauds as it applies in most states, including California, since the NDA has a term longer than one year. Certain contracts must be in writing to be enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1624 requires that certain contracts, including agreements that by their terms are not to be performed within a year from the making thereof, are invalid unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party's agent. As this agreement lasts for five years, it needs to be written and therefore oral notice is insufficient.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1624. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1624. (a) The following contracts are invalid, unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party’s agent: (1) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof. (2) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another, except in the cases provided for in Section 2794. (3) An agreement for the leasing for a longer period than one year, or for the sale of real property, or of an interest therein; such an agreement, if made by an agent of the party sought to be charged, is invalid, unless the authority of the agent is in writing, subscribed by the party sought to be charged. (4) An agreement authorizing or employing an agent, broker, or any other person to purchase or sell real estate, or to lease real estate for a longer period than one year, or to procure, introduce, or find a purchaser or seller of real estate or a lessee or lessor of real estate where the lease is for a longer period
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by requiring oral notice only, directly contradicts California Civil Code Section 1624(a)(1), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1. The original text requires written notice, which aligns with the Statute of Frauds, but the modified text eliminates this requirement, making the agreement unenforceable because it is not to be performed within a year. The law states that such agreements are invalid unless they are in writing. The modification removes a core legal obligation, making the contract non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: The laws of the State of YOUR COMPANY STATE shall govern the validity, construction and performance of this Agreement, provided, however , that no choice of law or rule of the State of YOUR COMPANY STATE , or any other jurisdiction, which would cause any such matter to be referred to the law of any jurisdiction other than YOUR COMPANY STATE shall be given any force or effect.
perturbation.changed_text: The laws of the State of North Korea shall govern the validity, construction and performance of this Agreement, provided, however, that no choice of law or rule of the State of North Korea, or any other jurisdiction, which would cause any such matter to be referred to the law of any jurisdiction other than North Korea shall be given any force or effect.
perturbation.explanation: Selecting the laws of North Korea is contrary to public policy because North Korea's legal system is not transparent, does not offer adequate due process, and does not protect fundamental rights that are recognized in the United States and many other jurisdictions. US courts would almost certainly refuse to enforce a contract governed by North Korean law due to public policy concerns.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 90 states that no action will be entertained on a foreign claim if it is so contrary to the strong public policy of the forum that it violates basic notions of justice and morality. Using North Korean law, which lacks basic protections of fairness and justice, would violate fundamental US public policy.

perturbation.justification: The changed text designates North Korean law to govern the agreement, which is contrary to U.S. public policy due to North Korea's lack of a transparent legal system, due process, and protection of fundamental rights. The Law Explanation, referencing Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 90, states that no action will be entertained on a foreign claim if it violates basic notions of justice and morality, which is exactly the case here. The original text stipulated the laws of YOUR COMPANY STATE, which is drastically different from North Korean law, leading to a clear contradiction. Although the snippets do not explicitly state this, the Law Explanation combined with the Perturbation explanation clearly indicates a legal contradiction that would make the contract unenforceable.
